data.title: 小さな勝利
data.text: When I can't get motivated, I start with the smallest possible task: making my bed, washing one dish, replying to one email. It sounds trivial, but something changes after that first completed action. The task was small; the momentum is not. Psychologists call this the progress principle—the idea that the feeling of moving forward, even slightly, fuels further effort. I call it tricking myself into a good day. Either way, it works, and it starts with one dish.

data.title: 雨の日のための蓄え
data.text: My grandmother kept an envelope of cash taped behind a picture frame. She called it her rainy day fund. As a child I thought it was a hiding game. As an adult, I understand it was peace of mind, purchased in small installments over years. Modern advice says money should never sit idle—invest it, grow it, make it work. All true. But there's a kind of return no investment offers: the ability to sleep soundly knowing that a broken refrigerator or a sudden trip will be an inconvenience, not a crisis. Some money's job is simply to be there.

data.title: 注意の予算
data.text: We budget money carefully and spend attention carelessly, though attention is the scarcer resource. Every morning you wake with a limited supply of focus, and everything around you is engineered to take a piece of it: notifications, headlines, other people's emergencies. By evening, the budget is spent, often on things you wouldn't have chosen deliberately. I started treating attention like money—asking 'is this worth it?' before giving my focus away. Some subscriptions got canceled. Some apps got deleted. Some people got shorter answers. What remained was attention for the things I actually chose, which, it turns out, is what being intentional means.

data.title: 第二言語の自分
data.text: Researchers have found that people sometimes make different moral choices depending on which language a dilemma is presented in—more utilitarian in a second language, more emotional in their first. I feel this in small ways. In Japanese, I am indirect, careful, attuned to what isn't said. In English, I am blunter, quicker to disagree, more comfortable taking up space. Neither is a performance; both are me. A second language isn't just a new set of words for old thoughts. It's a slightly different self, and switching between them is less like translation and more like changing rooms in the same house.

data.title: 完璧な瞬間を待たない
data.text: I spent years waiting for the right time to start things: the right time to write, to travel, to have difficult conversations. The right time never arrived. What arrived instead were slightly wrong times—too busy, too tired, too uncertain—and eventually I learned to start anyway. The conditions for beginning are almost never good. The people who accomplish things aren't the ones who found perfect moments; they're the ones who used imperfect ones. Waiting for readiness is just fear wearing a sensible disguise.

data.title: 受け継いだ味
data.text: My mother taught me to make curry the way her mother taught her. There is no recipe written down. You add 'enough' onion and cook it 'until it looks right.' For years my curry tasted wrong, and I couldn't ask why—the knowledge wasn't in words. Then one day it tasted exactly right, and I realized my hands had learned what my head couldn't. Now I understand why she never wrote it down. Some knowledge can only be passed from hands to hands.

data.title: デジタル移住
data.text: Last year I moved my digital life the way one moves apartments: I took only what I actually used. New laptop, and instead of transferring everything, I installed programs only when I needed them. After six months, I had installed eleven applications. My old laptop had ninety-four. The same principle applied to files—I copied documents only when I reached for them. Most of my digital possessions, like most physical ones, existed because removing them required a decision while keeping them required nothing. The move flipped the default, and the default, as always, decided everything.

data.title: 褒め方の技術
data.text: Good praise is specific. 'Great job' evaporates in seconds; 'the way you handled that angry customer without raising your voice was masterful' lasts for years. I know because I still remember specific compliments from a decade ago, while a thousand generic ones have vanished. Specific praise proves the person actually saw what you did. It says: I was paying attention, and what you did was worth attention. That's what people are starving for—not flattery, but evidence of having been truly seen. It costs one extra sentence. It's the cheapest generosity there is.
